title: Endpoints are declared communication objects
context: Local development workflows often involve local application URLs, callback URLs, and service URLs that must remain correctly routed to the owning worktree. Without explicit modeling, communication misdirection can occur when two worktrees run at the same addresses.
decision: In 1.0, endpoints are modeled as declared communication objects rather than raw derived addresses. Each endpoint declaration must include an endpoint name, an endpoint provider, and an intended role. In 1.0, one endpoint declaration maps to exactly one derived address per worktree instance. Endpoints are limited to local communication addresses relevant to the local runtime in 1.0.
alternatives: Treat endpoints as anonymous derived values rather than declared objects.
Unify resources and endpoints into a single concept.
Infer endpoints from application configuration rather than requiring explicit declaration.
rationale: Endpoints protect against communication misdirection rather than state collision. Modeling endpoints as declared objects preserves routing intent and ownership boundaries more clearly than treating them as anonymous derived values. Limiting endpoints to local communication addresses in 1.0 keeps the model focused.
consequences: The repository must declare endpoints explicitly.
Endpoint providers remain separate from resource providers.
The core tool preserves a business distinction between resource isolation and endpoint routing correctness.
reversal_trigger: If the endpoint declaration model creates friction for common routing scenarios that cannot be addressed within the current model, the model may be extended post-1.0.
